description: If A leads to B and B leads to C, then A leads to C.

hints.text: This is the **hypothetical syllogism**: transitivity of [implication|implication]. Given $A \to B$ and $B \to C$, you can chain them to get $A \to C$. Start by assuming the [conjunction|conjunction] of the two [premise|premises].
Split the [conjunction|conjunction] into its two halves with `and-elim-l` and `and-elim-r`.
Extract $A \to B$ from the left side.
Extract $B \to C$ from the right side.
You have a chain ready. Now you need to prove $A \to C$, which means: assume $A$ and reach $C$. Start by assuming $A$.
Use [modus-ponens|modus ponens] on $A \to B$ (step 2) and your fresh $A$ (step 4) to get $B$.
[modus-ponens|Modus ponens] again with $B \to C$ (step 3) and your newly-derived $B$ (step 5) to reach $C$.
